coredata.dc:title: Ultrasensitive electrochemiluminescence detection of p53 gene by a novel cloth-based microfluidic biosensor with luminol-gold nanoparticles and hybridization chain reaction amplification
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Hongyang
Su, Yan
Jiang, Jun
Liang, Yi
Zhang, Chunsun
coredata.dc:description: A dual-signal-amplified cloth-based ECL biosensor has been firstly explored to detect p53 gene, where the luminol functionalized gold nanoparticles (Lu-AuNPs) are used for probe labels and signal amplification, and the hybridization chain reaction (HCR) is further applied to increase the sensitivity of ECL detection. Carbon ink/solid wax-based screen-printing is utilized to manufacture the cloth-based carbon electrodes and sample/auxiliary chambers to construct a collapsible three-dimensional (3-D) cloth-based device. Profiting from the 3-D device feature, it is easy to prepare the sensing interface modified by multi-walled carbon nanotubes. When the target DNA, signal nanoprobes and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) are successively dropped onto the sensing interface, the Lu/H2O2-based ECL can be operated well. Under optimal conditions, the p53 gene can be desirably detected, with a linear range of 0.0001–1000 pM and detection limit of 0.02 fM. The biosensor has a wider linear range, and relatively high sensitivity, selectivity, stability and reproducibility, possibly because of the excellent constitution of signal nanoprobes and HCR products. Peculiarly, the biosensor has the capacity for p53 gene detection in the complex cellular homogenate samples, and discrimination of one-, two- and even three-base-mismatched p53 DNA sequences. So, it may supply a hopeful method in applications of gene diagnostics.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cloth-based microfluidics
Electrochemiluminescence
Luminol functionalized gold nanoparticles
Hybridization chain reaction
p53 tumor suppressor gene

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: ECL assay procedure and data analysis
Characterization of Lu-AuNPs and signal nanoprobes
Performance of the proposed biosensor for p53 gene detection
Selectivity, reproducibility and stability of the proposed biosensor
Determination of p53 gene in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the cloth-based ECL biosensor's preparation and p53 gene target assay procedure (MWCNTs-multi-walled carbon nanotubes; CS-chitosan; GA-glutaraldehyde; CP-DNA–NH2–amino-modified capture probe DNA; BSA-BB-BSA blocking buffer; T-DNA-target DNA; Lu-AuNPs-luminol functionalized gold nanoparticles; Lu-AuNPs-S1/Lu-AuNPs-S2-stem-loop DNA-modified Lu-AuNPs; HCR-hybridization chain reaction).
Characterization of as-prepared nanomaterials. (A) TEM images of Lu-AuNPs. (B) UV–vis spectra of luminol (a), HAuCl4 (b), AuNPs (c), Lu-AuNPs (d) and Lu-AuNPs-S1 (or Lu-AuNPs-S2) (e).

Characterization of cloth-based devices. (A) Photo of a typical screen-printed cloth-based device (WE-working electrode; RE-reference electrode; CE-counter electrode). (B–E) SEM images of bare cloth, wax screen-printed cloth, carbon ink screen-printed cloth, and MWCNTs–CS–modified electrode. (F) CV curves of different modified electrodes with a three-electrode cloth-based device (working solution-0.1 M KCl solution containing 10 mM K3 [Fe(CN)6]/K4 [Fe(CN)6]; scan rate-100 mV s−1; applied potential range: -1.2~1.2 V). (G) EIS curves of different modified electrodes with a conventional three-electrode system including a screen-printed carbon WE, Pt CE and Ag/AgCl RE (working solution-0.1 M KCl solution containing 10 mM K3 [Fe(CN)6]/K4 [Fe(CN)6]; frequency range: 0.1–100 kHz; amplitude: 5 mV). (H) Comparison of the ECL intensities with different device designs and H2O2 addition ways (Experiment conditions: [T-DNA]-500 pM, [CP-DNA-NH2]-0.5 μM, [S1]/[S2]-2 μM, tT-C-30 min, tHCR-60 min, scan rate-250 mV s−1 and [H2O2] −20 mM). Error bars represent the standard deviations of five independent measurements.

Dependence of ECL intensities on [CP-DNA-NH2] (A), [S1]/[S2] (B), incubation time between the T-DNA and CP-DNA (tT-C) (C), HCR time (tHCR) (D), scan rate (E) and [H2O2] (F). In panel (A), [T-DNA]-50 pM, [CP-DNA-NH2]-0.2–0.7 μM, [S1]/[S2]-2 μM, tT-C-30 min, tHCR-60 min, scan rate-250 mV s−1 and [H2O2]-20 mM. In panel (B), [T-DNA]-50 pM, [CP-DNA-NH2]-0.6 μM, [S1]/[S2]-1.6–2.1 μM, tT-C-30 min, tHCR-60 min, scan rate-250 mV s−1 and [H2O2]-20 mM. In panel (C), [T-DNA]-50 pM, [CP-DNA-NH2]-0.6 μM, [S1]/[S2]-2 μM, tT-C-20-50 min, tHCR-60 min, scan rate-250 mV s−1 and [H2O2]-20 mM. In panel (D), [T-DNA]-50 pM, [CP-DNA-NH2]-0.6 μM, [S1]/[S2]-2 μM, tT-C-40 min, tHCR-20-70 min, scan rate-250 mV s−1 and [H2O2]-20 mM. In panel (E), [T-DNA]-50 pM, [CP-DNA-NH2]-0.6 μM, [S1]/[S2]-2 μM, tT-C-40 min, tHCR-50 min, scan rate-50-300 mV s−1 and [H2O2]-20 mM. In panel (F), [T-DNA]-50 pM, [CP-DNA-NH2]-0.6 μM, [S1]/[S2]-2 μM, tT-C-40 min, tHCR-50 min, scan rate-200 mV s−1 and [H2O2]-1-25 mM. Error bars represent the standard deviations of five independent measurements.

(A) ECL intensities at different [T-DNA] values under optimized conditions ([T-DNA]-0, 0.0001, 0.0005, 0.005, 0.05, 0.5, 5, 50, 500, 1000, 2000, 4000, and 5000 pM). (B) Linear relationship between ECL intensities and logarithms of [T-DNA]. Error bars represent the standard deviations of five independent measurements.

Selectivity of the proposed ECL biosensor under optimized conditions. Here, the T-DNA and base-mismatched T-DNA sequence were in the same concentration (50 pM). The error bars represent the standard deviations of five independent measurements.

head.ce:title: Ultrasensitive electrochemiluminescence detection of p53 gene by a novel cloth-based microfluidic biosensor with luminol-gold nanoparticles and hybridization chain reaction amplification

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: MOE Key Laboratory of Laser Life Science & Institute of Laser Life Science, College of Biophotonics, South China Normal University, Guangzhou, 510631, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Laser Life Science, College of Biophotonics, South China Normal University, Guangzhou, 510631, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. MOE Key Laboratory of Laser Life Science, Institute of Laser Life Science & Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Laser Life Science, College of Biophotonics, South China Normal University, Guangzhou, 510631, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: MOE Key Laboratory of Laser Life Science
Institute of Laser Life Science & Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Laser Life Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A dual-signal-amplified cloth-based ECL biosensor has been firstly explored to detect p53 gene, where the luminol functionalized gold nanoparticles (Lu-AuNPs) are used for probe labels and signal amplification, and the hybridization chain reaction (HCR) is further applied to increase the sensitivity of ECL detection. Carbon ink/solid wax-based screen-printing is utilized to manufacture the cloth-based carbon electrodes and sample/auxiliary chambers to construct a collapsible three-dimensional (3-D) cloth-based device. Profiting from the 3-D device feature, it is easy to prepare the sensing interface modified by multi-walled carbon nanotubes. When the target DNA, signal nanoprobes and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) are successively dropped onto the sensing interface, the Lu/H2O2-based ECL can be operated well. Under optimal conditions, the p53 gene can be desirably detected, with a linear range of 0.0001–1000 pM and detection limit of 0.02 fM. The biosensor has a wider linear range, and relatively high sensitivity, selectivity, stability and reproducibility, possibly because of the excellent constitution of signal nanoprobes and HCR products. Peculiarly, the biosensor has the capacity for p53 gene detection in the complex cellular homogenate samples, and discrimination of one-, two- and even three-base-mismatched p53 DNA sequences. So, it may supply a hopeful method in applications of gene diagnostics.

ce:section.ce:para: The p53 gene, a tumor suppressor gene, plays crucial roles in the regulation of cell proliferation, cell differentiation, cell apoptosis, DNA repair and other biological functions [1,2]. This gene can encode and express the p53 protein that can control malignant transformation of cells, and thus it has a general anti-cancer effect and can be rightly defined as the genome guardian [3]. It is reported that approximately 50% of all malignancies contain the p53 gene mutation which has become the most common genetic transmutation in tumors of human beings [4]. Therefore, it is of great value to detect the p53 gene and its mutations with a sensitive, rapid and facile method. Up to now, a battery of measuring techniques have been reported for the p53 gene, including polymerase chain reaction single-strand conformational polymorphism (PCR-SSCP) analysis coupled with cell sorting [5] or DNA sequencing [6], fluorescence in situ hybridization (FISH) [7], high-performance liquid chromatography/electrospray ionization/tandem mass spectrometry (HPLC-ESI-MS/MS) [8], localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) [9], colorimetry [10], electrochemistry [11], chemiluminescence [12] and electrochemiluminescence (ECL) [13]. In these techniques, ECL has triggered widespread attention and has become a greatly promising DNA assay method, because it has the inherent superiorities of high sensitivity and selectivity, wide linear detection range, simple equipment, rapidness, and brilliant temporal and spatial control.

In the past few years, some groups have explored new ECL protocols for detection of p53 gene. For example, by coating the composite of multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) and Ru (bpy)3 2+ onto an electrode surface, Wang et al. have developed a solid-state ECL based on the enzyme method to detect the p53 gene [13]. Later, Wang et al. further exploited an ECL biosensor which used the hydrazide-modified graphene quantum dots (HM-GQDs) and hemin/G-quadruplex DNAzyme for sensitive detection of p53 gene [14]. Xu's group has presented a dual-potential ratiometric ECL sensing method for the p53 gene determination, where luminol and CdS nanocrystal are used as two emitters for ECL [15]. By using DNA probes-capped ion nanochannels, a label-free approach for ECL has been developed to detect DNA probe recognition event for p53 gene [16]. In this sensor, the nanochannel-structured membrane contains silica-sol, chitosan and Ru (bpy)3 2+. Cai and co-workers have reported an ECL tactics for detecting the methylation level and location of p53 gene, which utilized a conformation-switched hairpin DNA probe and ECL signal amplification strategy to improve the selectivity [17]. Lu et al. have presented the ECL resonance energy transfer between gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) and GQDs for the p53 single-stranded DNA damage detection [18]. Lin and co-workers developed a novel ECL biosensor, taking full advantage of hyperbranched rolling circle amplification and nicking endonuclease assisted target recycling to improve the selectivity and sensitivity for p53 gene detection [19]. Recently, Ma and co-workers have developed an ECL biosensor based on Au NPs@polydopamine@CuInZnS QDs nanocomposite for the surface plasmon resonance (SPR)-enhanced detection of p53 gene [20]. Additionally, a dichroic mirror-assisted ECL biosensor has been exploited for the simultaneous assay of mutant and wild-type p53 using photomultiplier tubes (PMTs), in which wild-type p53 is detected by using CdTe nanocrystals as tag in near-infrared wavebands and mutant p53 is detected by using CdSe nanocrystals as tag in eye-visible wavebands [21].

Despite the above-mentioned ECL methods exhibit desirable analytical performances for p53 gene, they still have some limitations as follows. Firstly, the commercial PMT-based analyzers are often used. The PMT detector has several inevitable shortcomings such as large dimension, high supply voltage and complicated machinery equipment. Secondly, the large-sized ECL chambers (usually several milliliters) are used, so more reagents and samples are needed. Thirdly, the bare electrodes often need to be polished and cleaned before use, and the pretreatment of non-disposable electrodes is complex. Fourthly, some biosensors have a more complicated sensing principle [18,21], probe design [14,17], or signal amplification [19]. Lastly, the vast majority of ECL assays have a low detection sensitivity, and the detection limits range from 10 fM to 3 nM [13,14,16–18,20,21]. Therefore, the wide applications of these ECL biosensors will be limited to some extent, especially in resource-poor settings.

To surmount the aforementioned shortcomings, we attempt to propose a new ECL biosensor in this paper. The screen-printed cloth-based chambers and carbon-electrodes are used to create the ECL environment for the detection of p53 gene, and the two simple signal amplification strategies are applied to improve the ECL sensitivity. As far as we know, this is the first time to develop a dual-signal-amplified cloth-based ECL biosensor for DNA detection. There are the several following attractive features for this novel ECL biosensor. Firstly, the as-prepared biosensors are inexpensive and disposable, eliminating the complicated pretreatment processes of the conventional ECL cell and electrodes. Secondly, the luminol functionalized AuNPs (Lu-AuNPs) are used as probe labels and signal amplification. The AuNPs have an excellent labeling property, and a single AuNP can bind multiple luminol molecules and sulfhydryl functionalized stem-loop DNA molecules. Besides, the AuNPs have the advantages of easy preparation, good biocompatibility, and promising optical and catalytic properties. Thirdly, the hybridization chain reaction (HCR) [22] is creatively combined with the Lu-AuNPs-based signal amplification to further enhance the detection sensitivity. The HCR which is a non-enzyme isothermic amplification technique can be operated at mild conditions, and has the low background, high amplification efficiency, and PCR-like sensitivity. Finally, by using the low-cost, hand-held charge coupled device (CCD) as the detector, the presented biosensor exhibits wide linear dynamic range, high selectivity and sensitivity, and receivable stability and reproducibility for the p53 DNA sequence. Especially, benefitting by two well-coupled signal enhancement strategies, the obtained limit of detection (LOD) can be low to 0.02 fM, which holds the potential for the early diagnosis of cancer development or ultrasensitive DNA determination required in some biomedical assays.

In order to prepare the mixture of MWCNTs and chitosan (CS) (MWCNTs-CS), 1 g MWCNTs were firstly submerged in 40 mL compound of HNO3 and H2SO4 (1:3, v/v), and sonicated at indoor temperature for 4 h in an ultrasonic cleaner. The obtained mixture was iteratively centrifuged and swilled with deionized (DI) water until the pH got neuter. Afterwards, the acid-treated MWCNTs were obtained by centrifugal filtrating and 24 h drying in an 80 °C oven. Then, 5 mg of acid-treated MWCNTs were dissolved in 1 mL of CS solution (2.5 g/L in acetate buffer, pH 6.0). After sonication, the MWCNTs-CS solution was obtained, and stored at indoor temperature.

To prepare the Lu-AuNPs, the used glasses were soaked for 12 h in the freshly-prepared HNO3/HCl mixture (3:1, v/v), and then cleaned with DI water and dried before use. Next, 50 mL HAuCl4 (0.01%, w/w) was stirred and heated until it boiled, and then 1.6 mL luminol solution (10 mM) was rapidly added under stirring and kept boiling for 30 min. Meanwhile, the color of the solution turned from light yellow to wine red. After that, the heating was stopped, while the stirring was continued at indoor temperature for 6 h. Next, the resulting solution was centrifuged for 5 min at 12,500 rpm, and cleaned three times with DI water to eliminate the impurities. Finally, the precipitate was afresh dissolved in DI water, and the obtained Lu-AuNPs were stored at 4 °C for ready-to-use.

200 μL thiol-modified stem-loop DNA1 or DNA2 (S1 or S2) (2 μM) was mixed with 5 μL tris(2-carboxyethyl)phosphine (0.1 M), and was pretreated at indoor temperature for 1 h to promote the binding of thiol groups and AuNPs. After that, the mixture solution was added into 1 mL as-prepared Lu-AuNPs solution, and the resulting solution was stirred at indoor temperature for 6 h and kept at 4 °C for 24 h to insure the firmness of the Au–S bond. Subsequently, the product (labeled as Lu-AuNPs-S1 or Lu-AuNPs-S2) was centrifuged at 12,500 rpm for 5 min to dislodge redundant reagents, and was resuspended in 1 mL signal probe buffer and stored at 4 °C.

Simply speaking, the chamber-screen and electrode-screen were patterned and custom-made. By using such screens, the sample chamber (Φ 6.5 mm) and working electrode (WE) could be constructed on one side of the 2-D cloth-based device, whereas the auxiliary chamber (Φ14 mm), counter electrode (CE) and reference electrode (RE) were situated on another side. Based on the electrode-screen, the carbon ink could be printed onto the cloth to get the electrode-patterned cloth, which was roasted for 30 min at 90 °C in the oven and then cooled to the indoor temperature. Thereafter, the chamber-screen was put above the backside of the electrode-patterned cloth, and a crayon and a smoothing appliance were utilized to wipe the screen in sequence to cause solid wax onto the cloth. Subsequently, the cloth and chamber-screen were placed in a heating board, and heated for ~3 s at 110 °C to fuse the printed wax into the cloth to form the hydrophobic barriers. Such hydrophobic barriers were used to demarcate the hydrophilic sample and auxiliary chambers. The sample chamber and auxiliary chamber were situated on the two sides of the fold-line (~1 mm width). Notably, the fold-line, which was not printed in the middle, could assemble two chambers to structure a 3-D cloth-based device. After folding, the three electrodes on the two chambers could be well coupled together to constitute the three-electrode ECL system.

After manufacturing the cloth-based device, the WE was modified to be functioned as the DNA biosensor. Above all, 2.5 μL MWCNTs-CS solution was added on the electrode and dried at indoor temperature (labeled as MWCNTs-CS/WE) (Step 1 in Fig. 1 ). After that, the electrode was modified by 2 μL glutaraldehyde (GA) (2.5% in phosphate buffer solution (PBS), v/v) for 2 h at room temperature, followed by in turn being laundered three times with PBS and air-dried at indoor temperature for ~30 min to obtain the GA/MWCNTs–CS–modified electrode (labeled as GA/MWCNTs-CS/WE) (Step 2 in Fig. 1). The aldehyde groups were introduced onto the WE by covalent combination of GA and CS. Notably, the use of GA is based on the following considerations: (1) GA can act as the cross-linking and enhance the stability of the CS film [23,24], and (2) the cost of the GA process is much lower than that required using the DNA modified with a carboxyl group. Then, the modified electrode was treated with 5 μL amino-modified capture probe DNA (CP-DNA-NH2) (0.5 μM in TE buffer) for 30 min at 37 °C in a constant temperature and humidity chamber. After probe immobilization, the WE was washed three times with the washing buffer to get rid of the superfluous CP-DNA-NH2 (labeled as CP-DNA/GA/MWCNTs-CS/WE) (Step 3 in Fig. 1). Next, 2 μL BSA blocking buffer (BSA-BB) (0.1 wt%) was applied onto the CP-DNA-modified electrode for 30 min at room temperature to block other active sites to avoid non-specific binding. After flushing three times by PBS, the cloth-based DNA biosensor could be procured (labeled as BSA-BB/CP-DNA/GA/MWCNTs-CS/WE) (Step 4 in Fig. 1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: ECL assay procedure and data analysis
ce:section.ce:para: 5 μL target DNA (T-DNA) was applied onto the DNA biosensor, and hybridized with the CP-DNA at 37 °C (Step 5 in Fig. 1). After hybridization for several tens of minutes, the electrode was swashed three times by the washing buffer, and was dried for ~10 min in air. Subsequently, the as-prepared Lu-AuNPs-S1 and Lu-AuNPs-S2 (5 μL) were respectively dropped onto the WE, and were incubated for a while at 37 °C to carry out the HCR (Step 6 in Fig. 1), followed by being laundered three times with the washing buffer to eliminate the excess reagents, and being dried in air for ~10 min. Then, by folding along the fold-line, the sample and auxiliary chambers could be well coupled together to form a biosensor based on the 3-D cloth-based device. After that, 40 μL H2O2 was added onto the auxiliary chamber on the back of the 3-D sensing device (Step 7 in Fig. 1). Under the action of capillary force, the two hydrophilic chambers could be well filled with H2O2 solution. Finally, the 3-D device was put in the portable ECL measurement and analysis system. Fig. S1 shows the schematic diagram of the circuit connection and the analysis system for the proposed ECL biosensor. The working potential from −2 V to 2 V was applied, and the triggered ECL signals were real-time recorded by the CCD. The data collection and analysis are attainable in supplementary information (SI).

To prove the HCR process, native polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis was applied to characterize the HCR. The sequences of T-DNA, S1 and S2 are shown in Table S1. It could be discerned from Fig. S2 that the DNA ladder marker, T-DNA, S1, S2, T-DNA + S1, S1+S2, T-DNA + S1+S2, and T-DNA + Lu-AuNPs-S1+Lu-AuNPs-S2 displayed different bands in gel electrophoresis, respectively. As expected, T-DNA, S1 and S2 showed a single bright band (lanes 1–3). If T-DNA and S1 were incubated together, a new downstream band was clearly observed because T-DNA could initiate the cross-opening of S1 to generate double-stranded DNA (lane 4). However, a new band disappeared when S2 was incubated with S1 (Lane 5). This indicated that S1 and S2 could relatively steadily coexist in defect of T-DNA. However, when T-DNA was mixed with S1 and S2, some distinct bands of the products were observed at the top of the strip (lane 6), suggesting that T-DNA, S1 and S2 were well hybridized to achieve HCR amplification. When the mixture of two signal nanoprobes (Lu-AuNPs-S1 and Lu-AuNPs-S2), instead of the mixture of S1 and S2, was applied to blend with T-DNA, a band of DNA polymer with higher molecular weight exhibited at the top of lane 7, implying the successful initiation of HCR by S1 and S2 on the surface of AuNPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of Lu-AuNPs and signal nanoprobes
ce:section.ce:para: The size and morphology of the Lu-AuNPs were characterized by transmission electron microscope (TEM). As shown in Fig. 2 A, the morphology of Lu-AuNPs was spherical (~20 nm in diameter), and moreover the dispersibility was good. Further, the UV–vis adsorption spectra were utilized to demonstrate the constitution of Lu-AuNPs and signal nanoprobes (Fig. 2B). The characteristic peaks of luminol solution were about at 300 nm and 350 nm (curve a). And, the spectra of the HAuCl4 and AuNPs (~20 nm, synthesized with sodium citrate as a reductant) solutions exhibited the characteristic peaks at about 290 nm and 530 nm, respectively (curves b and c). When the redox reaction between luminol and HAuCl4 was performed [25], the resulting NPs solution showed both the characteristic absorbance of luminol at 300 nm and 349 nm and the characteristic absorbance of AuNPs at 535 nm, which manifested that the luminol functionalized AuNPs had been synthesized (curve d). The high-density luminol on AuNPs is good for enhancing the ECL emission. As for the UV–vis spectrum of Lu-AuNPs-S1 (or Lu-AuNPs-S2), an obvious characteristic absorption peak of DNA located at 260 nm, suggesting that S1 (or S2) could be successfully connected onto Lu-AuNPs via Au–S bond (curve e).

It can be seen from Fig. 3 A that the ordinal carbon ink- and wax-based screen-printing processes could afford a receivable hydrophobic/hydrophilic distinction for the cloth-based device. Especially, after three electrodes were one-step screen-printed onto the cloth, the sample and auxiliary chambers also could be one-step manufactured onto the cloth to desirably accommodate these electrodes if the chamber-screen was placed on the back of the electrode-patterned cloth. Notably, the screen-printed cloth-based devices were not expensive. Based on the used materials, instruments and software, the assay cost of a single biosensor could be estimated to only be $0.136 (Table S2). Figs. 3B-D show the scanning electronic microscope (SEM) pictures of different functionalized zones on the cloth-based device. For the bare cloth (i.e. unwaxed cloth chambers), the numerous gaps among the fibers (~10 μm in diameter) could be obviously observed, manifesting that an acceptable hydrophilic environment could be supplied (Fig. 3B). Nevertheless, because of the cloth's porous structure, the screen-printed wax could commendably permeate into the cloth to form the hydrophobic barrier (Fig. 3C). For cloth-based carbon electrodes, a dense and consecutive carbon conducting layer was created on the cloth surface, demonstrating that the carbon materials could desirably cover upon the cloth cellulose fiber's surfaces (Fig. 3D). It can be seen from Fig. 3E that a continuous and narrow meshed MWCNTs layer could form on the WE surface, which may be attributed to the satisfying capacity of MWCNTs-CS to form the film on the electrode [26].

The cyclic voltammetry (CV) (Fig. 3F) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) (Fig. 3G) were utilized to further demonstrate the modified electrodes. As shown in Fig. 3F, the MWCNTs-CS/WE had a much higher current magnitude than the bare WE (curves a and b), implying that MWCNTs-CS with high specific surface area and superior conductivity promoted the electron transfer. However, when GA was anchored onto the modified electrode (GA/MWCNTs-CS/WE), the peak current decreased due to the intervention of GA to the electroactive probe (curve c). Further, the current decreased significantly after the CP-DNA was immobilized on the electrode (CP-DNA/GA/MWCNTs-CS/WE) (curve d), showing that the negative charges of CP-DNA restrained the redox probe from arriving the electrode. A further current decrease was found when BSA-BB was used to obstruct non-specific active sites (curve e), because of the prevention of electron transport from the BSA protein. When the modified electrode (BSA-BB/CP-DNA/GA/MWCNTs-CS/WE) was incubated with the T-DNA solution, a noticeable decrease in peak current was observed (curve f). The possible reason for this case was that the T-DNA molecules on the electrode impeded the electron transport, and the hybridization products had a bigger steric hindrance. Finally, both Lu-AuNPs-S1 and Lu-AuNPs-S2 were applied onto the T-DNA-modified electrode (T-DNA/BSA-BB/CP-DNA/GA/MWCNTs-CS/WE), the peak current farther shortened (curve g), suggesting that the signal nanoprobes were immobilized onto the electrode to induce the HCR. Moreover, the surface variations of the modified electrodes were researched by the EIS method (Fig. 3G). The outcomes of the EIS were alike to those of the CV, suggesting the triumphant assembly of the proposed biosensor as anticipated.

The ECL property of the biosensor was used to evaluate the luminescence responses of the modified electrode with different device designs and H2O2 addition ways (Fig. 3H). In our case, before folding of the 2-D cloth-based device, the surface on which the WE is located is defined as the front surface of the cloth-based device. For columns a and b in Fig. 3H, the WE was screen-printed on the front surface of the device, while the RE and CE were screen-printed on the back surface of the device. For columns c and d in Fig. 3H, all three ECL electrodes were screen-printed on the front surface of the device. As shown in Fig. 3H, the ECL intensities in the case of addition of H2O2 onto the auxiliary chamber were slightly higher than those obtained on addition of H2O2 on the sample chamber. By comparing columns a and b (or columns c and d), it can be found that the ECL intensities of the thin and rectangle RE were higher than those of the round RE. The possible reason for two abovementioned phenomena is that the H2O2 coreagent in the former cases could well fill into the auxiliary and sample chambers under the capillarity. In addition, it has been observed that the ECL intensities obtained when all three electrodes were printed on the front surface (columns c and d) were correspondingly higher than those obtained when the WE was screen-printed on the front surface and the other two electrodes were screen-printed on another surface (columns a and b). For the former, the spatial configuration of three electrodes, especially the distance between WE and other electrodes, may be more reasonable.

To acquire a better response of cloth-based ECL, several detection conditions were optimized. Firstly, the influence of the concentration of CP-DNA-NH2 on the ECL signal was investigated. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the ECL intensity strengthened with the CP-DNA-NH2 concentration from 0.2 to 0.6 μM, and then decreased when the concentration exceeded 0.6 μM. Once higher density of CP-DNA was embellished onto the WE, the resulting steric hindrance might prevent more T-DNA from being hybridized with the CP-DNA [26]. Similarly, the concentrations of S1 and S2 were optimized, where the S1/S2 concentration ratio was controlled to be 1:1. It can be seen from Fig. 4B that the ECL intensity enhanced with the increasing concentrations of S1 and S2, and reached an approximate plateau after 2.0 μM. Thus, the optimized concentrations of CP-DNA-NH2 and S1/S2 were chosen to be 0.6 μM and 2.0 μM, respectively.

As we know, the detection sensitivity depended on the T-DNA-associated reactions on the electrode surface. Therefore, the incubation time between the T-DNA and CP-DNA (tT-C), and as well the HCR time (tHCR) were optimized. As shown in Fig. 4C, the ECL intensity tended to the maximum when the tT-C value was 40 min, which was ascribed to the restriction of the saturated hybridization site [27]. Thus, 40 min was used for the subsequent hybridization time. Furthermore, the ECL signal had no distinct variation after the HCR time arrived at 50 min (Fig. 4D). The possible reason for this is that longer reaction time decreased the folding free energy, which ultimately caused the termination of chain hybridization events [28]. Based on this fact, 50 min was chosen as the optimal tHCR value.

In general, the ECL behavior is highly associated with the generation rate of excited-state species during the electron-transfer process [20]. When the scan rate ranged from 50 to 200 mV s−1, the ECL intensity gradually increased because of the generation of more electro-generated radical species within short time span (Fig. 4E). However, the ECL intensity decreased when the scan rate was over 200 mV s−1. This may be because a higher scan rate was unsuited for electrooxidation of luminol to induce radical species, and the electrochemical process was not reversible [20,29]. So, 200 mV s−1 was selected to make the following experiments.

For the luminol ECL system, the concentration of the co-reactant H2O2 has intense influence on the ECL signal. It can be seen from Fig. 4F that the ECL intensities gradually heightened with the H2O2 concentrations and arrived at a maximum at 15 mM, which may be caused by the co-oxidation function of H2O2. However, the ECL intensity fell off when the H2O2 molarity was higher than 15 mM, due to the decrease of the ion index of the substrate solution [30,31]. Therefore, 15 mM H2O2 was used in further experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of the proposed biosensor for p53 gene detection
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimal conditions, the linear range and detection limit of the presented biosensor were evaluated. It can be seen from Fig. 5 A that the ECL intensity augmented when the gene concentration ranged from 0 to 1000 pM, and remained almost constant with further increasing the [T-DNA] from 2000 to 5000 pM. The typical ECL image corresponding to each signal value is shown in Fig. S3. It has been found that the ECL intensity revealed good linear relation with the logarithm of [T-DNA] ranging from 0.1 fM to 1000 pM (Fig. 5B). The LOD was calculated to be 0.02 fM, which was referred to the concentration producing an ECL signal three standard deviations higher than the blank. It should be noted that compared with most of previous ECL biosensors for p53 gene detection, the proposed ECL biosensor has the preferable sensitivity and linear range, and only requires simple and low-cost manufacture technologies and equipments (Table S3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity, reproducibility and stability of the proposed biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The biosensor's selectivity was evaluated by performing the hybridization experiments for various DNA sequences including the T-DNA, single-base mismatched T-DNA1 or T-DNA2 (SMT-DNA1 or SMT-DNA2), double-base mismatched T-DNA (DMT-DNA), three-base mismatched T-DNA (TMT-DNA), noncomplementary T-DNA (NCT-DNA), and blank solution (Blank). The detailed information of DNA sequences is listed in Table S1. As shown in Fig. 6 , the ECL signal using the NCT-DNA sequence was similar to that of the Blank, and moreover it gradually increased with decreasing the mismatched base number. However, the SMT-DNA1 and SMT-DNA2 with single mismatch generated a much lower ECL intensity than that of T-DNA. These results proved the high selectivity of the designed ECL biosensor.

Practically speaking, the reproducibility and stability of the biosensor were another two essential elements for quantification of p53 DNA sequence. Here, the reproducibility was assessed by relative standard deviations (RSDs) of intra-assay and inter-assay which were evaluated with the ECL intensities of 50 pM T-DNA. The calculation method and process for the RSD were as follows. The five ECL intensity values were used to get the average and the standard deviation, and then the standard deviation was divided by the average and multiplied by 100% to obtain the RSD. As a result, the T-DNA assay using five biosensors fabricated with the same batch of cloth-based devices (intra-assay) showed an RSD of 5.86%. For the assay using five different biosensors fabricated independently on different batches of cloth-based devices (inter-assay), the RSD was calculated to be 4.48%. These results indicated that the proposed ECL biosensor had a good manufacture reproducibility and precision. In the present case, the intra-assay exhibited a slightly higher RSD than the inter-assay, which is similar to the cases reported in some previous studies [26,32–34]. The reason for this phenomenon may be that the screen-printing process of cloth-based devices has little effect on the RSDs of the intra-assay and inter-assay, but the RSDs are mainly affected by some other operations or processes such as modification of the WE, addition of reaction solutions, on-device incubation reactions, signal detection and so on. To evaluate the storage stability, the biosensors were hermetic and put at 4 °C, and then they were tested every two days. As shown in Fig. S4, a small shift in ECL response could be seen within the period of 22-day. After ten, sixteen and twenty-two days, the biosensors could keep 96.4%, 89.5% and 84.5% of their initial intensity, respectively. Therefore, the presented biosensor possessed a receivable stability of storage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of p53 gene in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To study the application feasibility and ability of the established ECL biosensor, p53 DNA sequences were included into normal human liver cellular homogenate samples (1.6 × 106 cells/mL), and then their concentration levels were measured under the optimized conditions. The results could be observed in Table S4. The recoveries ranged from 94.06% to 107.33%, and the RSDs were less than 5%, indicating that the proposed biosensor may have the ability to determine the real samples and to turn into an alternative means for clinical diagnostic applications.

To sum up, we have proposed for the first time the integration of HCR, Lu-AuNPs-based ECL and cloth-based microfluidics for the DNA sequence quantification. Especially, the used dual-signal-amplified strategy can greatly decrease the ECL background (as clearly seen in the optimization results), and thus improve the detection performance. The results have shown that under optimal conditions the ECL biosensor can be desirably applied for the p53 gene detection in the range of 0.1 fM to 1000 pM, with the LOD as low as 0.02 fM. In addition, the presented biosensor demonstrates remarkable selectivity, reproducibility and stability, and as well admissible application capacity in the real sample test. It can be foreseen that the proposed ECL method can be extended for other biomedical applications or serve as the basis of sensing other biological recognition events.

Hongyang Wu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Resources, Data curation, Writing - original draft, Visualization. Yan Su: Methodology, Investigation, Resources. Jun Jiang: Methodology, Investigation, Visualization. Yi Liang: Methodology, Investigation. Chunsun Zhang: Conceptualization, Data curation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (81571765), and Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation (2019A1515011284). We thank Dr. Bowen Shu at Guangzhou First People's Hospital for his help in providing the normal human liver cellular homogenate samples.
